chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I must reason only from high-level crash metadata.

Top native frame is strlen_default, indicating a crash in strlen, typically due to invalid/non-terminated pointer. The frame below is Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode in libDSToolkitV30Jni.so, which is the first app-level native frame.

This JNI method decodes Base64; its inputs are almost certainly attacker-controlled data (here, fuzz input) coming from Java. A strlen crash in such a function almost always means it called strlen (directly or via C library) on data that was not guaranteed to be NUL-terminated or was otherwise an invalid pointer.

The JNI name suggests it operates on a Java byte[] or String and probably converts it to a C string. A common bug is using GetByteArrayElements/GetStringUTFChars and then assuming an internal NUL terminator or mis-using length (e.g., calling strlen on a buffer that may contain zero bytes or lacks a terminator).

Because I cannot decompile libDSToolkitV30Jni.so, I cannot see exact argument flows into strlen, but the combination of (1) strlen_default at the top, (2) Base64 decode native routine, (3) fuzz harness successfully crashing it, and (4) no evidence that the JNI layer tightly constrains the data, strongly indicates a genuine memory-safety bug rather than a harness-only issue.

The crash is likely reachable in the real app: Base64 decoding of arbitrary server or user input is common in crypto/security libraries; ReaderBasedJsonParser._decodeEscaped() is being fuzzed, but in normal operation it will eventually call Base64.decode on data derived from JSON content, which can be attacker-controlled via network or file. There is no reason to think that production Java code would never pass malformed data; malformed JSON or bad Base64 is realistic.

Therefore this is a likely vulnerability: unsafe use of C string APIs on attacker-controlled data inside a JNI Base64 decoder, causing out-of-bounds read and potential info leak or DoS. Exploitability for code execution is less clear (strlen read OOB tends to be read-only), so severity is better modeled as medium/high depending on context; I’ll mark high given memory corruption potential (invalid pointer or long over-read into unmapped memory).
Due to lack of binary/Java decompilation, confidence cannot be 0.9+, but the pattern is strong enough for ~0.7–0.75.
reasons: Crash occurs in strlen_default called from JNI Base64 decode routine, indicating strlen on invalid or unterminated data
Base64 decode input is attacker-controlled (from fuzz harness and plausibly from JSON/user data in production), so unsafe C-string handling is influenced by untrusted input
No evidence the crash is due exclusively to an artificial harness condition; the scenario (malformed/unterminated Base64 data) is realistic in real-world app use

evidence.note: Top native frame where process terminates, typical of reading past valid memory when given bad pointer or non-terminated buffer
First app-native frame below strlen_default; Base64 decode of attacker-controlled data likely passes invalid pointer/unterminated buffer to strlen
Java-side path shows this can be reached from JSON parsing on external data

recommendations: Decompile Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode and audit all uses of strlen/strcpy/strcat and similar to ensure they do not operate on buffers lacking explicit length and NUL termination
Refactor Base64 decode implementation to use length-aware APIs (e.g., track explicit buffer length rather than relying on strlen) and add strict bounds checking on all attacker-controlled data
Add targeted tests/fuzzing around Base64 decode JNI entry to reproduce this crash under controlled inputs and validate the fix
assumptions: Base64 input originates from potentially untrusted JSON or external data in real app flows, not only from internal constants
The harness passes data to Java in a way consistent with realistic app usage (malformed strings but not impossible JNI contracts)
limitations: Could not decompile libDSToolkitV30Jni.so or inspect exact implementation/argument flow to strlen
No exact crash address or register state to prove whether the pointer was NULL, invalid, or just unterminated
Java sources around Base64 usage and JSON handling were not directly inspected; reachability inferred from provided call graph and typical library behavior
